title: State-of-Charge Estimation via Coulomb Counting
content: Coulomb counting integrates measured current over time to track accumulated charge removed from or added to a battery. SoC(t) = SoC(t₀) − (1/Q_nominal) × ∫I(t) dt, where positive I is discharge and Q_nominal is rated capacity in Ah.
extended_content: Coulomb counting is inherently drift-prone because small current measurement errors accumulate. Calibration resets are performed at known endpoints: full charge (SoC = 100 %) or full discharge (SoC = 0 %). Current is measured with a shunt resistor or Hall-effect current sensor (e.g., ACS712, INA219). INA219 (I²C): 12-bit ADC, ±3.2 A (with 0.1 Ω shunt), 1 mA resolution; reads current and bus voltage. LiFePO₄ cells have a very flat discharge curve (3.2–3.3 V under load for 80 % SoC), making voltage-only SoC estimation inaccurate — Coulomb counting is preferred. Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) methods combine OCV and Coulomb counting for higher accuracy but require a cell model. Practical accuracy: ±3–5 % SoC with regular calibration resets.
source: Plett, Battery Management Systems Vol. 1, Artech House, 2015; Texas Instruments BQ34Z100 TRM
